NEW DELHI -(Dow Jones)- Enron Corp. (ENE) said it has entered into a definitive 
agreement to sell its stake in the Dabhol Power Co. (DPC) power project in 
India to the Maharashtra State Electricity Board (MSEB). 
The company said it has agreed to sell its 25% stake in the project to the MSEB 
for $1.5 billion. 
The sale is subject to a number of conditions, including the payment of $1.5 billion in 
unpaid bills and the payment of $1.5 billion in accrued interest. 
Enron will also have to pay $1.5 billion in termination fees to the MSEB. 
The company said it expects to close the sale by the end of the year.

The challenge was to ensure that employees followed the social media guidelines and that the company's social media accounts were not used to promote the company's products or services. 
Enron's social media team, led by Sara Shackleton, developed a set of guidelines for employees to follow. 
The guidelines included:
* No direct or indirect promotion of the company, products or services
* No use of the company's trademarked or copyrighted names, logos, or images
* No use of the company's website or any other Enron-related website
* No use of the company's telephone number or e-mail address
* No use of the company's fax number or e-mail address
* No use of the company's Internet address (enron.com)
* No use of the company's e-mail address (enron.messaging.administration@enron.com)

By Peter Eavis

10/27/2001
Reuters English News Service
(C) Reuters Limited 2001.

NEW YORK, Oct 27 (Reuters) - Shares of Enron Corp. fell $1.50 to $17.50 in heavy trading on Wall Street on Monday, after the energy trader's bankruptcy filing triggered a wave of buybacks by energy companies. 
"It's a buyback tsunami," said one trader. 
Enron's shares fell $1.50 to $17.50 in heavy trading on the New York Stock Exchange, after the Houston-based company said it would seek to restate its financial statements and seek protection from creditors under Chapter 11 of the U.S. Bankruptcy Code. 
The company's shares have fallen more than 80 percent since Oct. 16, when it disclosed that it had lost $1.2 billion in the third quarter, and that it may have to restate its financial statements.

NEW YORK, Oct 27 (Reuters) - The bankruptcy filing of Enron Corp. could trigger a wave of buyouts, analysts said on Monday. 
"It's a big deal. It's a big deal for the energy industry," said one energy trader. 
"It's going to be a big deal for the energy industry. It's going to be a big deal for the energy industry," said another energy trader. 
The Houston-based energy trader said it was too early to tell whether the bankruptcy filing would trigger a wave of buyouts. 
"It's too early to tell. It's too early to tell," said the trader. 
Enron, the largest energy trader, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection on Monday, saying it had lost $1.2 billion in the last two weeks.

"It was a little bit of a shock," says Mr. Kowalke. "We were
thinking, 'What's going on here?' "

The answer, of course, is that the company's stock price had fallen so
far that it was no longer worth the trouble of courting customers.

The company's stock price has since recovered, but not by much. In the
past year, the company has lost $1.2 billion.

The lesson, says Mr. Kowalke, is that "you have to be very, very
careful about the stock price."
